{{Vertical_images_list | Align = | Width = 220px | Frame_width = 220px | 1 = Misodetenmangu-kaidan 01.JPG | 2 = Stone steps (featured in films such as ''The Girl Who Leapt Through Time'') | 3 = Misode-tenmangu06n3872.jpg | 4 = Worship Hall | 5 = Misode-tenmangu03n3872.jpg | 6 = Shrine Gate }}
'''Misode Tenmangū''' (御袖天満宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan. It is a Tenmangū shrine enshrining [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] and was classified as a former [[Gōsha]] under the premodern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]], revered as the god of learning and scholarship.
== History == In 901 CE, during his exile to [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] following accusations from [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], Sugawara no Michizane landed in Onomichi. Locals offered him barley rice and sweet sake. In gratitude, Michizane tore a sleeve from his garment, painted his likeness on it, and gave it to the people. During the late Enkyū era (1069–1074), a shrine was built at the site to enshrine this relic as "Misode no Mikage" ("Divine image of the Sleeve"), leading to the shrine's name.
The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''[[:en:The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time]]'' and the TV drama ''[[:en:Teppan|Teppan]]''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''[[:en:Kamichu!|Kamichu!]]''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Onomichi Station|Onomichi Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://misodetenmangu.or.jp/ Official website]

== Access == - A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Onomichi Station|Onomichi Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Misode-tenmangu]][[ja:御袖天満宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489320]] Wikidata name: Misode Tenmangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Misode Tenmangū | image = [[File:Misode-tenmangu01n3872.jpg|300px|Torii gate]] | location = 1-11-16 Nagae, Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|24|48.5|N|133|12|07.8|E|region:JP-34_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Sugawara no Michizane | Shrine_Rank = Former Gō-sha (郷社) | founded = Late 11th century (Enkyū era) | Main_building = Irimoya-zukuri, copper roof | Pilgrimage = [[25 Sites Sacred to Sugawara no Michizane]] | annual_festival = Friday to Sunday preceding July 25 }}
{{Vertical_images_list | Align = | Width = 220px | Frame_width = 220px | 1 = Misodetenmangu-kaidan 01.JPG | 2 = Stone steps (featured in films such as ''The Girl Who Leapt Through Time'') | 3 = Misode-tenmangu06n3872.jpg | 4 = Worship Hall | 5 = Misode-tenmangu03n3872.jpg | 6 = Shrine Gate }}
'''Misode Tenmangū''' (御袖天満宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan. It is a Tenmangū shrine enshrining [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] and was classified as a former [[Gōsha]] under the premodern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]], revered as the god of learning and scholarship.
== History == In 901 CE, during his exile to [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] following accusations from [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], Sugawara no Michizane landed in Onomichi. Locals offered him barley rice and sweet sake. In gratitude, Michizane tore a sleeve from his garment, painted his likeness on it, and gave it to the people. During the late Enkyū era (1069–1074), a shrine was built at the site to enshrine this relic as "Misode no Mikage" ("Divine image of the Sleeve"), leading to the shrine's name.
The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''[[:en:The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time]]'' and the TV drama ''[[:en:Teppan|Teppan]]''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''[[:en:Kamichu!|Kamichu!]]''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Onomichi Station|Onomichi Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://misodetenmangu.or.jp/ Official website]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Misode-tenmangu]][[ja:御袖天満宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489320]] Wikidata name: Misode Tenmangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Misode Tenmangū | image = [[File:Misode-tenmangu01n3872.jpg|300px|Torii gate]] | location = 1-11-16 Nagae, Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|24|48.5|N|133|12|07.8|E|region:JP-34_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Sugawara no Michizane | Shrine_Rank = Former Gō-sha (郷社) | founded = Late 11th century (Enkyū era) | Main_building = Irimoya-zukuri, copper roof | Pilgrimage = [[25 Sites Sacred to Sugawara no Michizane]] | annual_festival = Friday to Sunday preceding July 25 }}
{{Vertical_images_list | Align = | Width = 220px | Frame_width = 220px | 1 = Misodetenmangu-kaidan 01.JPG | 2 = Stone steps (featured in films such as ''The Girl Who Leapt Through Time'') | 3 = Misode-tenmangu06n3872.jpg | 4 = Worship Hall | 5 = Misode-tenmangu03n3872.jpg | 6 = Shrine Gate }}
'''Misode Tenmangū''' (御袖天満宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan. It is a Tenmangū shrine enshrining [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] and was classified as a former [[Gōsha]] under the premodern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]], revered as the god of learning and scholarship.
== History == In 901 CE, during his exile to [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] following accusations from [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], Sugawara no Michizane landed in Onomichi. Locals offered him barley rice and sweet sake. In gratitude, Michizane tore a sleeve from his garment, painted his likeness on it, and gave it to the people. During the late Enkyū era (1069–1074), a shrine was built at the site to enshrine this relic as "Misode no Mikage" ("Divine image of the Sleeve"), leading to the shrine's name.
The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''[[:en:The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time]]'' and the TV drama ''[[:en:Teppan|Teppan]]''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''[[:en:Kamichu!|Kamichu!]]''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Onomichi Station|Onomichi Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://misodetenmangu.or.jp/ Official website]

{{Vertical_images_list | Align = | Width = 220px | Frame_width = 220px | 1 = Misodetenmangu-kaidan 01.JPG | 2 = Stone steps (featured in films such as ''The Girl Who Leapt Through Time'') | 3 = Misode-tenmangu06n3872.jpg | 4 = Worship Hall | 5 = Misode-tenmangu03n3872.jpg | 6 = Shrine Gate }}
'''Misode Tenmangū''' (御袖天満宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan. It is a Tenmangū shrine enshrining [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] and was classified as a former [[Gōsha]] under the premodern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]], revered as the god of learning and scholarship.
== History == In 901 CE, during his exile to [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] following accusations from [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], Sugawara no Michizane landed in Onomichi. Locals offered him barley rice and sweet sake. In gratitude, Michizane tore a sleeve from his garment, painted his likeness on it, and gave it to the people. During the late Enkyū era (1069–1074), a shrine was built at the site to enshrine this relic as "Misode no Mikage" ("Divine image of the Sleeve"), leading to the shrine's name.
The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''[[:en:The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time]]'' and the TV drama ''[[:en:Teppan|Teppan]]''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''[[:en:Kamichu!|Kamichu!]]''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Onomichi Station|Onomichi Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''[[:en:The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time]]'' and the TV drama ''[[:en:Teppan|Teppan]]''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''[[:en:Kamichu!|Kamichu!]]''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Onomichi Station|Onomichi Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]

{{Vertical_images_list | Align = | Width = 220px | Frame_width = 220px | 1 = Misodetenmangu-kaidan 01.JPG | 2 = Stone steps (featured in films such as ''The Girl Who Leapt Through Time'') | 3 = Misode-tenmangu06n3872.jpg | 4 = Worship Hall | 5 = Misode-tenmangu03n3872.jpg | 6 = Shrine Gate }}
'''Misode Tenmangū''' (御袖天満宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan. It is a Tenmangū shrine enshrining [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] and was classified as a former [[Gōsha]] under the premodern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]], revered as the god of learning and scholarship.
== History == In 901 CE, during his exile to [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] following accusations from [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], Sugawara no Michizane landed in Onomichi. Locals offered him barley rice and sweet sake. In gratitude, Michizane tore a sleeve from his garment, painted his likeness on it, and gave it to the people. During the late Enkyū era (1069–1074), a shrine was built at the site to enshrine this relic as "Misode no Mikage" ("Divine image of the Sleeve"), leading to the shrine's name.
The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''[[:en:The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time]]'' and the TV drama ''[[:en:Teppan|Teppan]]''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''[[:en:Kamichu!|Kamichu!]]''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Onomichi Station|Onomichi Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]

The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''{{ill|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|en|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|WD=Q336056|lt=The Girl Who Leapt Through Time}}'' and the TV drama ''{{ill|Teppan|en|Teppan|WD=Q15948631|lt=Teppan}}''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''{{ill|Kamichu!|en|Kamichu!|WD=Q972136|lt=Kamichu!}}''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Onomichi Station|en|Onomichi Station|WD=Q861547|lt=Onomichi Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]

{{Vertical_images_list | Align = | Width = 220px | Frame_width = 220px | 1 = Misodetenmangu-kaidan 01.JPG | 2 = Stone steps (featured in films such as ''The Girl Who Leapt Through Time'') | 3 = Misode-tenmangu06n3872.jpg | 4 = Worship Hall | 5 = Misode-tenmangu03n3872.jpg | 6 = Shrine Gate }}
'''Misode Tenmangū''' (御袖天満宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan. It is a Tenmangū shrine enshrining {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} and was classified as a former [[Gōsha]] under the premodern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, revered as the god of learning and scholarship.
== History == In 901 CE, during his exile to {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|WD=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu}} following accusations from {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|en|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara no Tokihira|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}, Sugawara no Michizane landed in Onomichi. Locals offered him barley rice and sweet sake. In gratitude, Michizane tore a sleeve from his garment, painted his likeness on it, and gave it to the people. During the late Enkyū era (1069–1074), a shrine was built at the site to enshrine this relic as "Misode no Mikage" ("Divine image of the Sleeve"), leading to the shrine's name.
The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''{{ill|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|en|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|WD=Q336056|lt=The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|1=The Girl Who Leapt Through Time}}'' and the TV drama ''{{ill|Teppan|en|Teppan|WD=Q15948631|lt=Teppan|1=Teppan}}''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''{{ill|Kamichu!|en|Kamichu!|WD=Q972136|lt=Kamichu!|1=Kamichu!}}''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Onomichi Station|en|Onomichi Station|WD=Q861547|lt=Onomichi Station|1=Onomichi Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://misodetenmangu.or.jp/ Official website]

== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''{{ill|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|en|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|WD=Q336056|lt=The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|1=The Girl Who Leapt Through Time}}'' and the TV drama ''{{ill|Teppan|en|Teppan|WD=Q15948631|lt=Teppan|1=Teppan}}''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''{{ill|Kamichu!|en|Kamichu!|WD=Q972136|lt=Kamichu!|1=Kamichu!}}''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Onomichi Station|en|Onomichi Station|WD=Q861547|lt=Onomichi Station|1=Onomichi Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Misode Tenmangū | image = [[File:Misode-tenmangu01n3872.jpg|300px|Torii gate]] | location = 1-11-16 Nagae, Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|24|48.5|N|133|12|07.8|E|region:JP-34_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Sugawara no Michizane | Shrine_Rank = Former Gō-sha (郷社) | founded = Late 11th century (Enkyū era) | Main_building = Irimoya-zukuri, copper roof | Pilgrimage = [[25 Sites Sacred to Sugawara no Michizane]] | annual_festival = Friday to Sunday preceding July 25 }}
{{Vertical_images_list | Align = | Width = 220px | Frame_width = 220px | 1 = Misodetenmangu-kaidan 01.JPG | 2 = Stone steps (featured in films such as ''The Girl Who Leapt Through Time'') | 3 = Misode-tenmangu06n3872.jpg | 4 = Worship Hall | 5 = Misode-tenmangu03n3872.jpg | 6 = Shrine Gate }}
'''Misode Tenmangū''' (御袖天満宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Onomichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan. It is a Tenmangū shrine enshrining {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} and was classified as a former [[Gōsha]] under the premodern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, revered as the god of learning and scholarship.
== History == In 901 CE, during his exile to {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|WD=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu}} following accusations from {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|en|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara no Tokihira|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}, Sugawara no Michizane landed in Onomichi. Locals offered him barley rice and sweet sake. In gratitude, Michizane tore a sleeve from his garment, painted his likeness on it, and gave it to the people. During the late Enkyū era (1069–1074), a shrine was built at the site to enshrine this relic as "Misode no Mikage" ("Divine image of the Sleeve"), leading to the shrine's name.
The shrine was historically associated with [[Daisen-ji Temple]], which oversaw its operations until the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the Meiji era. It was designated a gōsha during the modernization of the shrine Ranking system.
Legends of Michizane's travels along the Seto Inland Sea are widespread, with some suggesting that a wealthy family, the Kanaya, may have influenced the shrine's narrative to enhance their prestige. The Kanaya family, prominent during the Edo period, sponsored commemorative monuments and owned fields where the barley for sake was reportedly grown.
== festivals == The annual ''Tenjin festival'' is held over three days, typically from the Friday to Sunday before July 25. Events include: - **Day 1**: Mikoshi (portable shrine) procession - **Day 2**: Academic prayer festival and community activities such as raffles and performances - **Day 3**: A grand procession featuring the mikoshi ascending and descending the 55 stone steps.
== Shrine Buildings == - **main hall (Honden)**: Reconstructed in 1983 following a fire in 1973, it retains an irimoya-zukuri style with a copper roof. - **Worship Hall (Haiden)**: Features a gabled roof with a projecting eave. - **Shrine Gate (Shinmon)**: An 8-legged gate with a tiled roof.
== Grounds == The shrine is accessed via 55 stone steps, with the final step intentionally made of joined stones. At the base of the stairs is a resting stone where Michizane is said to have paused. The grounds also feature a "stroking ox" statue.
The shrine has been featured in multiple media, including the film ''{{ill|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|en|The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|WD=Q336056|lt=The Girl Who Leapt Through Time|1=The Girl Who Leapt Through Time}}'' and the TV drama ''{{ill|Teppan|en|Teppan|WD=Q15948631|lt=Teppan|1=Teppan}}''. It also inspired settings in the anime ''{{ill|Kamichu!|en|Kamichu!|WD=Q972136|lt=Kamichu!|1=Kamichu!}}''.
== Access == - A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Onomichi Station|en|Onomichi Station|WD=Q861547|lt=Onomichi Station|1=Onomichi Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyō Main Line|Sanyō Main Line]] - A 5-minute walk from the "Nagaeguchi" bus stop served by [[Onomichi Bus]] and [[Chugoku Bus]] - A 5-minute walk from the base station of the [[:en:Senkoji Ropeway|Senkoji Ropeway]]
